  • Tutorials
  • Tutorial: Phase Change Materials (PCM)
  • Tutorial: Phase Change Materials (PCM)

Tutorial: Phase Change Materials (PCM)

Phase Change Materials store thermal energy by melting (absorbing heat) during warm periods and solidifying (releasing heat) during cold periods. They buffer temperature swings without consuming electricity.

How PCM works in a greenhouse

  Day (sunny):  T_air rises → PCM absorbs heat → melts (T stays at T_melt)
  Night:        T_air drops → PCM releases heat → solidifies → slows cooling

The PCM state is tracked by its enthalpy H [J/m²]:

PhaseTemperatureEnthalpy
SolidT < T_meltH < 0
MeltingT = T_melt (isothermal)0 ≤ H ≤ m·L
LiquidT > T_meltH > m·L

Available presets

PresetT_meltL_fusionMassLocationTechnology
ParaffinFloor()18°C250 kJ/kg15 kg/m²Under floorRT18HC (Rubitherm)
SaltHydrateWall()29°C190 kJ/kg3 kg/m²North wallCaCl₂·6H₂O
BioPCMContainers()21°C180 kJ/kg10 kg/m²ContainersCoconut oil / fatty acids
MicroencapsulatedCeiling()23°C110 kJ/kg5 kg/m²Ceiling panelsBASF Micronal

Usage

using GreenhousesJL

tmy = load_tmy("path/to/weather.txt")

# Paraffin under the floor
p = SavonaParams(tmy; with_walls=true, pcm=ParaffinFloor())

# Combine with other strategies
p = SavonaParams(tmy;
    with_walls = true,
    cover = ChineseCover(),          # night blanket
    walls = InsulatedNorthWall(),     # insulated north
    pcm = BioPCMContainers(),        # PCM containers
    crop = LettuceCrop(),            # lettuce with cycles
)

When PCM is most effective

PCM works by buffering temperature oscillations. It provides the most benefit when:

  1. No continuous heating (free-floating or frost protection only), the PCM smooths day/night swings
  2. High solar gain during the day, enough energy to melt the PCM
  3. Large day/night temperature range, Mediterranean, arid climates

In a greenhouse with tight PI control (constant 18°C), the PCM has little to buffer since the controller already eliminates temperature swings.

Simulation results (Savona 5×5m, Brussels Dec, 7 days)

Free-floating (no heating):

ConfigurationT_minT_maxSwing
No PCM−0.8°C18.0°C18.8°C
Paraffin Tm=18°C−0.2°C18.0°C18.2°C
Bio-PCM Tm=21°C−0.5°C18.0°C18.5°C

The paraffin raises the minimum temperature by 0.6°C, meaningful for frost protection.

With PI heating at 18°C:

ConfigurationEnergy saving
No PCMbaseline
Paraffin~0% (PI already controls temperature)

Custom PCM

# Define your own PCM
my_pcm = PCMStorage(
    22.0,       # T_melt [°C], choose close to your setpoint
    200e3,      # L_fusion [J/kg], latent heat of fusion
    20.0,       # m_per_m2 [kg/m²], mass per m² of floor
    1800.0,     # cp_solid [J/(kg·K)]
    2000.0,     # cp_liquid [J/(kg·K)]
    0.25,       # λ_pcm [W/(m·K)], thermal conductivity
    8.0,        # h_exchange [W/(m²·K)], convective coupling to air
    :floor,     # location
    "my custom PCM"
)

Monitoring PCM state

# After simulation, extract PCM enthalpy and temperature
H_pcm_idx = ...  # index depends on configuration (after soil layers)
H_pcm = sol[H_pcm_idx, :]

T_pcm = [pcm_temperature(H, p.pcm) for H in H_pcm]
liquid = [pcm_liquid_fraction(H, p.pcm) for H in H_pcm]

References

  • Berroug, F. et al. (2011). Thermal performance of a greenhouse with a phase change material north wall. Energy and Buildings, 43, 3027–3035.
  • Benli, H. & Durmuş, A. (2009). Performance analysis of a latent heat storage system with PCM for new designed solar collectors in greenhouse heating. Applied Energy, 86, 1913–1921.
  • Lazaar, M. et al. (2014). Use of a latent heat storage system for heating a greenhouse. Energy Conversion and Management, 88, 1069–1076.
  • Rubitherm Technologies GmbH. RT18HC datasheet.
